title: New in Together GPU Clusters: Reliability and control for production GPU clusters
gist: Together AI shipped a bundle of platform-health features (passive health checks, human-in-the-loop auto node repair, a rebuilt Slurm-on-Kubernetes stack based on its Slinky fork) and operational-control features (cluster details view, external OIDC for Kubernetes RBAC, self-serve startup scripts, opt-in acceptance testing) aimed at moving GPU Clusters customers beyond single-admin-kubeconfig, ticket-driven operations toward enterprise-grade multi-user, self-service infrastructure.

themes.theme: Passive health checks and human-in-the-loop node repair

insights.claim: Together AI's existing active health checks were synthetic tests run at provisioning time or on idle nodes, which could not catch failures that only manifest under real production workloads, motivating the buildout of new passive health checks that run continuously across every node observing live workloads, logs, and metrics.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Shows Together AI closing a real gap (load-dependent failures invisible to synthetic tests) that likely caused undetected production incidents for customers running multi-day training jobs -- a concrete reliability upgrade for buyers evaluating GPU cluster vendors.
insights.quote: Active checks are useful at provisioning time and on idle nodes. Passive checks extend that coverage to failures that appear while real workloads are running.
insights.locator: Catching and fixing failures as they happen

insights.claim: New passive health checks cover GPUs falling off the PCIe bus, thermal throttling, Xid errors, and Slurm node drains on failure, observing live workloads with near-zero overhead on running jobs.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Concrete list of failure classes now monitored gives a benchmark for comparing observability depth against other GPU cloud providers (CoreWeave, Lambda, etc.).
insights.quote: The coverage list today includes GPUs falling off the bus, thermal throttling, Xid errors, Slurm node drains on failure, and an expanding set of hardware and software failure signals.
insights.locator: Catching and fixing failures as they happen

insights.claim: Together AI paired passive health checks with auto node repair that generates a recommended remediation from four possible actions (reboot, reprovision, failover, remove-to-RMA) but requires an operator to approve the action rather than executing fully automatically.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: The human-in-the-loop design signals Together AI is deliberately trading automation speed for safety on high-value training/inference workloads -- a risk-management posture investors can compare against competitors that may auto-drain more aggressively.
insights.quote: Your training checkpoints and inference replicas are too valuable to risk on automated drains. Our 'human-in-the-loop' approach keeps you in control — the system detects and recommends, you approve, and Together handles the graceful recovery.
insights.locator: Catching and fixing failures as they happen

insights.claim: Together AI states it is already building fully automated node-repair options for specific failure modes as a next step beyond the current human-approval workflow.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Signals a roadmap toward reduced operational overhead for customers over time, which should show up as lower support-ticket volume and faster MTTR in future releases.
insights.quote: We’re already building fully automated node-repair options for specific failure modes, but for now, we prioritize keeping your production workloads uninterrupted.
insights.locator: Catching and fixing failures as they happen

insights.claim: Together AI rebuilt its Slurm-on-Kubernetes stack from its fork of the open-source Slinky project to fix recurring 'scale-at-failure' problems: crashing daemons, zombie processes holding GPU memory for days, and Slurm's GPU-state view drifting from actual hardware after pod reschedules.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Confirms Together AI is investing engineering effort in the open-source Slinky project for large-scale Slurm/Kubernetes training infra, relevant to competitive positioning against other managed Slurm-on-K8s offerings.
insights.quote: We've rebuilt our Slurm-on-Kubernetes stack from the ground up, based on our fork of OSS project Slinky, to make those 'scale-at-failure' headaches a thing of the past.

insights.claim: The rebuilt stack moves Slurm job accounting data (sacct history) from ephemeral storage to durable PVC-backed storage, so pod restarts no longer wipe the accounting database, preserving billing reconciliation and post-hoc job analysis.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Fixes a data-integrity issue that could have caused billing disputes or lost audit trails for enterprise customers -- material for trust/compliance evaluation.
insights.quote: sacct history used to live on ephemeral storage, which meant a pod restart could wipe your entire accounting database. We've moved accounting to durable, PVC-backed storage.

insights.claim: The new stack tracks every descendant process of a job at the kernel level and cleans them all up at job end, fixing a prior bug where daemonized child processes could hold GPU memory and /dev/shm segments hostage for days after a job ended.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: This was a real GPU-utilization drag (wasted capacity from orphaned processes) that directly affected customer cost efficiency -- its fix is a quantifiable efficiency improvement for GPU cluster renters.
insights.quote: daemonized child processes used to slip out from under Slurm's view and stay running — holding GPU memory and /dev/shm segments hostage between runs, sometimes for days until someone cleaned them up by hand.

insights.claim: All newly provisioned Slurm clusters run on the new stack by default, and existing managed Slurm customers can be migrated in place at no additional cost via a scheduled maintenance window.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: No-cost migration removes friction/lock-in risk for existing GPU Clusters customers, relevant when assessing customer retention dynamics.
insights.quote: If you're on an existing managed Slurm cluster, we can migrate you in place at no cost via. a maintenance window — contact your account team to schedule.

themes.theme: Enterprise access control and self-service customization

insights.claim: Together AI added External OIDC support for Kubernetes RBAC, letting teams authenticate cluster access against their own identity provider (Google, Okta, Auth0, Microsoft Entra ID, or other OIDC-compatible IdPs) instead of sharing a single admin kubeconfig, enabling per-user audit trails and revocation.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: This is an enterprise-readiness feature signaling Together AI is moving upmarket to serve larger, security-conscious organizations that require per-user access control -- a leading indicator of larger contract sizes.
insights.quote: The admin kubeconfig becomes a break-glass tool. Joiners and leavers are handled in your IdP, where they should be.

insights.claim: Together AI shipped self-serve startup scripts that let customers run shell scripts at node boot, job start, and job end to customize Slurm worker/login/controller nodes, eliminating the prior need to SSH into every node manually or file a support ticket for common setup tasks.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Reduces vendor support burden and customer time-to-customization, an operating-leverage improvement for Together AI's own cost structure as it scales its customer base.
insights.quote: We were watching customers SSH into every node to do this by hand, or file support tickets and wait on us. Startup scripts move that setup into cluster configuration as a self-serve capability.

insights.claim: Together AI disables its full acceptance-test battery (GPU health, networking, storage validation) by default to prioritize faster time-to-availability for smaller or short-lived clusters, but recommends customers explicitly opt in to acceptance testing for large-scale or long-running training clusters where catching a bad node before epoch 47 is cheaper than catching it after.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Reveals an explicit engineering tradeoff between speed and validation that customers must actively manage; large training customers should always enable acceptance testing to avoid costly mid-run failures.
insights.quote: Catching a bad node at provisioning time is much cheaper than catching it on epoch 47, and the cost of validation is small relative to the lifetime of the cluster.
insights.locator: Acceptance testing, opt-in for larger and longer-running clusters

insights.claim: Together AI rebuilt its cluster overview page and added Nodes, Health checks, and Repair tabs so operators can answer 'is it healthy, is it being used, what happened recently' from the product UI instead of via a Slack thread with Together's support team.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Replacing informal Slack-based support with in-product observability is a scalability signal -- Together AI is productizing support interactions it previously handled manually, improving margins as its customer count grows.
insights.quote: Both exist for the incident retro question, “what actually happened on this cluster last week?”, and they replace what used to be a Slack thread with us.

insights.claim: Together AI states its ongoing investment priorities are covering more failure modes with passive health checks, automating more remediation actions end-to-end, deepening node-level observability, and continued investment in its Slurm and Kubernetes stacks that most large training jobs depend on.
insights.speaker: Pavneet Ahluwalia
insights.so_what: Gives forward guidance on where Together AI's infrastructure engineering spend is headed, useful for tracking whether future releases follow through on stated roadmap.
insights.quote: The themes you’ll continue to see from us: more failure modes covered by passive health checks, more remediation actions safe to automate end-to-end, deeper observability into what’s actually happening on your nodes, and continued investment in the Slurm and Kubernetes stacks that most large training jobs depend on.
